coredata.dc:title: A photoelectrochemical sensor for ultrasensitive dopamine detection based on single-layer NanoMoS2 modified gold electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Hun, Xu
Wang, Shanshan
Wang, Shiyu
Zhao, Jikuan
Luo, Xiliang
coredata.dc:description: Photoactive material is a crucial factor which affects the sensitivity and selectivity of the photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor. A simple PEC sensing platform was fabricated using nanoMoS2 modified gold electrode (nanoMoS2/GE) in this paper. Three kinds of nanoMoS2, single-layer nanoMoS2, multiple-layers nanoMoS2 and small-sized nanoMoS2, were used to modify the electrode. It was found that the single-layer nanoMoS2 showed utmost PEC response. The photocurrent of nanoMoS2/GE was drastically improved upon the addition of dopamine, which was attributed to the life improvement of the electron-hole pairs. Based on the enhanced photocurrent signal, a PEC methodology for ultrasensitive determination of dopamine has been developed, which showed linearly enhanced photocurrent by increasing the dopamine concentration from 1.0×10−11 M to 1.0×10−5 M with a detection limit of 2.3×10−12 M and the RSD was 3.9% at 2.0×10−10 M (n=9) under optimal conditions. This method showed high sensitivity, simplicity and wide linearity range which opened up a new promising signal-on PEC platform for small molecule detection in bioanalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical
Sensor
Dopamine
NanoMoS2
Gold electrode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the modified electrodes and PEC measurements
Effect of concentration of nanoMoS2 on PEC intensity
Effect of pH on PEC intensity
Reproducibility and stability of the photoelectrochemical platform
Mechanism of PEC analysis of dopamine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of nanoMoS2 PEC platform mechanism.
SEM images of single-layer nanoMoS2 (A), multiple-layers nanoMoS2 (B), small-sized nanoMoS2 (C).

PEC response of different nanoMoS2 modified gold electrode, which were measured in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 1μM dopamine. a, bare GE; b, small-sized nanoMoS2/GE; c, multiple-layers nanoMoS2/GE; d, single-layer nanoMoS2/GE.

The relationship between the concentration of single-layer nanoMoS2 and the photocurrent response, which were measured in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 1μM dopamine. The concentration of single-layer nanoMoS2 was 0.0625, 0.08, 0.5, 0.1, 0.3, 0.25, 0.125 and 0.2mgmL−1 from a to h, respectively.
Effect of pH on PEC signal. pH was 5.7, 6.0, 8.0, 6.5, 7.5 and 7.0 from a to f, respectively. The concentration of dopamine was 1μM.
Photocurrent response of different concentration of dopamine from 1.0×10−11 to 1.0×10−5 M; blank was performed in PBS (pH 7.0) without dopamine (n=9).
Comparison of nanoMoS2/GE photoelectrochemical platform for dopamine detection with other methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: g-C3N4, graphitic carbon nitride; TiO2, TiO2 nanoparticles; ITO, indium tin oxide; GQDs, graphene quantum dots; DMSA, Meso-2,3-dimercaptosuccinic acid; QDs, quantum dots; FTO, fluorine-doped tin oxide; Ag2Se QDs: water-dispersed Ag2Se quantum dots; ECL, Electrogenerated Chemiluminescence; MWCNTs-CoNPs, Multi-walled carbon nanotubes functionalized by cobalt nanoparticles; EC, Electrochemistry; ePAD, electrochemical paper-based analytical device; GSCR-MIPs, graphene sheets/Congo red-molecular imprinted polymers.

head.ce:title: A photoelectrochemical sensor for ultrasensitive dopamine detection based on single-layer NanoMoS2 modified gold electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker, Ministry of Education, State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-Chemical Engineering, Qingdao 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology
Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker
State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive and simple photoelectrochemical platform with nanoMoS2 modified gold electrode was fabricated. Comparing three kinds of nanoMoS2, single-layer nanoMoS2, multiple-layers nanoMoS2 and small-sized nanoMoS2, it was found that single-layer nanoMoS2 performs best PEC response for dopamine detection. The detection limit was 2.3×10−12 M.

Photoactive material is a crucial factor which affects the sensitivity and selectivity of the photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor. A simple PEC sensing platform was fabricated using nanoMoS2 modified gold electrode (nanoMoS2/GE) in this paper. Three kinds of nanoMoS2, single-layer nanoMoS2, multiple-layers nanoMoS2 and small-sized nanoMoS2, were used to modify the electrode. It was found that the single-layer nanoMoS2 showed utmost PEC response. The photocurrent of nanoMoS2/GE was drastically improved upon the addition of dopamine, which was attributed to the life improvement of the electron-hole pairs. Based on the enhanced photocurrent signal, a PEC methodology for ultrasensitive determination of dopamine has been developed, which showed linearly enhanced photocurrent by increasing the dopamine concentration from 1.0×10−11 M to 1.0×10−5 M with a detection limit of 2.3×10−12 M and the RSD was 3.9% at 2.0×10−10 M (n=9) under optimal conditions. This method showed high sensitivity, simplicity and wide linearity range which opened up a new promising signal-on PEC platform for small molecule detection in bioanalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) measurement has been wildly applied in bioanalytical chemistry as a newly emerging and dynamically developing analytical method owing to its low-cost, high sensitivity, wide linearity range, rapid detection and low back-ground current [1,2]. The mechanism of PEC is based on the oxidative capacity of photogenerated hole or reductive property of photoelectron and the photoelectrochemically active materials’ photogenerated electron transfer between the analyte and a semiconductor electrode [3,4]. In PEC detection process, light is used as the excitation source and current is obtained as the detection signal [5,6], which can be viewed as the inverse process of electroluminescence (ECL) [7,8]. Therefore, it can exhibit charge-transfer upon photoexcitation and achieve the detection purpose via the intensity of the photocurrent. A series of PEC platforms have been designed to detect small molecules [4,9–11].

Since the performance of the PEC sensors depends on the properties of the photoactive materials, there is much effort devoting to design and synthesize novel materials. Various active species, such as semiconducting nanoparticles [12,13], organic small molecules [14,15], metal complexes [16,17] and grapheme nanocomposites [8,18,19] have been investigated for PEC study to meet the particular demand. Among them, the graphene is an important martial due to its thermal and photochemical stability and highly applicable photophysical and redox properties. Most importantly, the material revolutionized the traditional ideology of nanoscience and nanotechnology and opened up a door to a new two-dimensional (2D) nanosystem. Usually, the 2D layered structure is beneficial to shorten the charge transport time and distance. So far, most of PEC platforms about grapheme nanocomposites have been formed [20,21].

Besides graphene, the 2D layered structure of nanoMoS2 is highlighted material nowadays ascribe to its outstanding properties such as excellent mechanical properties, optical properties and electronic properties [22]. The anisotropic layered structure of MoS2 leads to vastly different properties between the single-layer or multiple-layers flakes and the bulk material. For example, Zhu et al. designed a nucleic acid sequence detecting method with the single-layer MoS2 nanosheet as sensing platform which demonstrated that MoS2 nanosheet is biocompatible and suitable for DNA analysis in electrochemical measurements [23]. Wang et al. also reported a method for sensitive DNA detection with nanoMoS2 modified electrode [24]. In particular, the single-layer nanoMoS2 demonstrates its excellent semiconducting electronic properties as well as strong photocurrent with sensitive photoresponse [25,26].

To the best of our knowledge, there is no research based on MoS2 nanosheets for small molecule detection with PEC detection system. Inspired by these thoughts, a sensitive PEC detecting method with MoS2 nanosheet as the sensing platform was fabricated in this article. The nanoMoS2 was coated on the gold electrode (GE) to detect dopamine by using the PEC platform. Comparing three kinds of nanoMoS2, single-layer nanoMoS2, multiple-layers nanoMoS2 and small-sized nanoMoS2, it was found that single-layer nanoMoS2 performed best PEC response for ultrasensitive dopamine detection.

PEC measurements were performed with a homemade detection system. A 10W LED lamp was used as irradiation source (Fig. S1). Photocurrent was performed on a CHI 830D electrochemical working station (CHI instrument, USA) with a conventional three-electrode cell. A gold electrode (GE) or modified electrode was used as the working electrode, calomel electrode as reference electrode and a platinum wire as auxiliary electrode. The nanoMoS2 was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6700F machine, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope). UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired with a Shimadzu UV-3600 UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The nanoMoS2 were purchased from Nanjing Jcnano Technology Company (Nanjing, China). N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was provided by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and dopamine (DA) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water from an Aquapro ultrapure water system (Ever Young Enterprises Development. Co., Ltd. Chongqing, China). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was prepared by mixing the stock solutions of 0.2M NaH2PO4 and 0.2M Na2HPO4 containing 0.1M KCl. The unit mol L−1 also abbreviated as M in this paper.

The nanoMoS2 was pretreated via a simple ultrasound method before using. The detailed process can be described as follows: 10mg of nanoMoS2 was transferred into 20mL DMF and disposed with constant stirring for 5min. Followed by ultrasonication for 20min, the homogenous suspense of nanoMoS2 was formed. The production was washed with 1.0mL of 0.1M phosphate buffer solution three times with centrifugal separation, and resuspended in 1.0mL phosphate buffer solution and kept at 4°C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the modified electrodes and PEC measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Before modification, the GE was polished with 1, 0.3, 0.05μm alumina slurries sequentially and washed ultrasonically with doubly distilled water. Then it was electrochemically cleaned in 0.5M H2SO4 solution by cyclic potential scanning between 0.3 and 1.5V until a standard CV was obtained. Subsequently, the GE was rinsed with doubly distilled water and absolute ethanol in turn. After dried with nitrogen gas, 10μL of nanoMoS2 solution was dripped onto the fresh GE surface and dried naturally in the air. The obtained electrode was denoted as nanoMoS2/GE. The PEC activity of the nanoMoS2 modified was investigated by Amperometric i-t curves in 0.1M PBS containing dopamine during the photocurrent measurement irradiated with white LED light. The light was switched on and off every 10s, and the potential applied was 0.0–0.5V (vs SCE). After the background photocurrent became stable, the PEC response was recorded.

NanoMoS2 is known for the layered structure material. In this paper, we used three species different nanoMoS2 to investigate their PEC response. NanoMoS2 TEM showed some layered nanosheets (Fig. 1 ) which were the typical morphological features of nanoMoS2 [27]. The single-layer nanoMoS2 were of ultrathin nanosheet morphology (Fig. 1a), which owned the utmost transparence compared with the multiple-layers nanoMoS2 (Fig. 1b) and the small-sized nanoMoS2 (Fig. 1c). The multiple-layers nanoMoS2 showed much more sheets than the single-layer nanoMoS2. The diameter of small-sized nanoMoS2 was about 300nm and showed shatter morphology. However, the single-layer nanoMoS2 is about several hundred micrometers long similarly to that of the multiple-layers. SEM images of nanoMoS2 were shown in Fig. S2. From the SEM image, we can see that the nanoMoS2 were of nanosheet morphology. Single-layer nanoMoS2 was few layers. Multiple-layers nanoMoS2 was overlapping each sheet. Small-sized nanoMoS2 was flake, which were consistent with the TEM results. Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) was also used for the characterization of nanoMoS2. The analysis results showed the three kinds of nanoMoS2 were all formed with molybdenum and sulfur at the ratio of 1:2, which was consistent with the typical MoS2 [27]. In this paper, we chose the single-layer nanoMoS2 to modify GE. And the SEM of the single-layer nanoMoS2/GE was also provided in Fig. S3. From Fig. S3, it can be seen that electrode was covered with nanoMoS2 symmetrically. It certified that the modified gold electrode was successfully prepared. Fig. S4 showed the typical UV–vis spectra of nanoMoS2. The absorption spectrum of nanoMoS2 supernatants displayed excellent absorption of light in the visible radiation region, which was corresponding to the typical absorption spectrum of MoS2 nanosheets [28]. It also shows two peaks with maxima at 628 and 674nm, attributed to the characteristic A and B direct excitonic transitions of MoS2 respectively [29]. While the white LED used in this paper emits visible radiation [30]. This ensures nanoMoS2 effectively absorb the light form white LED lamp.

In order to guarantee sensitivity and stability, there were a series of experiments to investigate the influences of the different morphology of nanoMoS2. The PEC activity of the nanoMoS2 modified gold electrode was investigated in PBS (pH 7.0) containing 1μM dopamine using a typical three-electrode configuration coupling with homemade optical system at room temperature. As is shown in Fig. 2 , the nanoMoS2/GE (curve b, c, d) performed a remarkable photocurrent comparing to bare GE (curve a). The bare GE showed low photocurrent. After modification with the nanoMoS2, the prepared nanoMoS2/GE exhibited remarkable photocurrents response, which illustrated that the nanoMoS2 had enhanced PEC activity. The experimental results indicated that PEC signal of the three kinds of nanoMoS2 modified is 4–9 times than that of bare GE. In comparison with the multiple-layers nanoMoS2/GE and the small-sized nanoMoS2/GE, the single-layer nanoMoS2/GE (curve d) showed the utmost PEC response. The signal values was about 923nA, while the multiple-layers one (curve c) and the small-sized one (curve b) performed smaller signal values 709nA and 496nA. The reason why for this phenomenon was possible that the single-layer nanoMoS2 demonstrated its excellent semiconducting electronic properties as well as strong photocurrent with sensitive photoresponse compared with that of the multiple-layers and the small-sized. Consequently, the single-layer nanoMoS2 modified gold electrode was used for further investigation. Control experimental was performed. It was found that in the dopamine solution PEC signal was higher than that in the PBS without dopamine (Fig. S5). It confirmed that dopamine could enhance PEC response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of concentration of nanoMoS2 on PEC intensity
ce:section.ce:para: To better perform photocurrent, the influence of the concentration of nanoMoS2 on the PEC platform was investigated in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 1μM dopamine. As shown in Fig. 3 , the photocurrent response of nanoMoS2/GE was increased significantly with increasing the concentration of nanoMoS2 from 0.0625mgmL−1 to 0.2mgmL−1. Nevertheless, when the concentration of nanoMoS2 exceeded 0.2mgmL−1, the photocurrent response decreased. The reason for this phenomenon may be ascribe to the fact that a film was formed by excessive nanoMoS2 covered on the GE which was too much heavy to the charge deliver between dopamine and GE. It reduced the visible light absorption. So 0.2mgmL−1 was chosen as the optimal concentration for subsequent experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on PEC intensity
ce:section.ce:para: Similarly, in order to obtain the excellent PEC signal response, pH of phosphate buffer solution was also investigated. From Fig. 4 , it showed the photocurrent intensity was increased slightly when pH increased from 5.7 to 6.5. Specially, when pH increased from 6.5 to 7.0, the photocurrent intensity was increased dramatically. However, the photocurrent intensity started to decline when pH exceeded 7.0. It is attributed to the deprotonation of dopamine above pH 7.0 and is attributed to the protonation below pH 7.0 [31,32]. So pH 7.0 was used.

Under the optimum condition, the quantitative detection of dopamine was conducted. A series of concentrations of dopamine was performed using the photoelectrochemical electrodes immobilized with nanoMoS2. The photocurrent increased gradually with the increasing concentration of dopamine, which implied the obtained PEC sensor was appropriate for dopamine detection. The photocurrent intensity increased with increasing concentration of dopamine ranging from 1.0×10−11 M to 1.0×10−5 M (Fig. 5 ). Blank values were performed in PBS (pH 7.0) without dopamine. The regression equation was ΔI=0.1709 log (C/M)+1.8850 with a regression coefficient of 0.998. ΔI=I–I0, where I0 or I was the photocurrent of nanoMoS2/GE with PBS in the absence or presence of dopamine. The detection limit is 2.3×10−12 M (S/N=3) and the RSD is 3.9% at 2.0×10−10 M (n=9). Compared with some reported PEC methods for dopamine determination, this work showed lower detection limit (Table 1 ). The detection limit of this method was 10 or 300 times lower than that of CdS QDs modified ITO [33] or GQDs-TiO2 modified GCE [34] detecting dopamine. Also, there were a wide linear range of concentrations of this electrode as a promising photoelectrochemical sensor.

To evaluate the practical application of this PEC platform, the selectivity of this platform for dopamine determination was assessed. The selectivity of the PEC sensor based on nanoMoS2 modified GE is tested by measuring the photocurrent response for commonly chemical or biological interferences including ascorbic acid (AA), cysteine (Cys) and uric acid (UA) with the concentration of 1.0×10−10 M. These compounds showed much smaller or negligible signals compared to that of dopamine with the concentration of 5.0×10−11 M, revealing the high selectivity of this sensor for the dopamine detection (Fig. S6). The results demonstrated the fabricated photoelectrochemical sensor possessed better specificity and selectivity for dopamine detection. This result is consistent with that in the reported literature [42]. It is perhaps attributed to the low potential being used in the detection system. In order to take the selectivity of the PEC biosensor into consideration, a bias potential of 0V was selected for the detection of dopamine using PEC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the photoelectrochemical platform
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility of this method was also investigated through the parallel measurement method. Nine-made nanoMoS2/GEs were used to detect 2.0×10−10 M dopamine and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.9%. The results indicated that the reproducibility of this method was satisfactory. NanoMoS2 modified gold electrode also exhibited good long term stability, which was demonstrated by measuring the photocurrents obtained at the PEC biosensors over a successive period of time. It observed that 97.2%, 94.5% and 89.3% of the initial photocurrents were retained after the PEC biosensors were stored at 4°C for 1, 2 and 4 weeks respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of PEC analysis of dopamine
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrated the procedure of the fabrication of PEC sensor and the PEC platform mechanism. NanoMoS2 was coated onto the fresh GE with the adsorption. The bare GE showed low photocurrents (Fig. 2). After fictionalization with the nanoMoS2, the prepared nanoMoS2/GE exhibited remarkable increased photocurrents which illustrated that the nanoMoS2 had enhanced PEC activity. Dopamine is a monoamine neurotransmitter distributed in the central neural system brain tissues and body fluids of mammals. It plays pivotal roles in the function of the central nervous, renal, hormonal and cardiovascular system. So, we chose dopamine as the detecting subject. The photocurrent increased further after adding dopamine. We can conjecture that under visible light irradiation, the photoactive material nanoMoS2 absorbs photons with energies higher than that of its band gap [43]. Because the anodic positive experimental parameter was set, the down PEC cure and anodic photocurrent was obtained. It confirmed that the electrons in the valence band are excited to the conduction band, forming the electron-hole pairs. The electrons then are transferred to GE, generating photocurrent [44]. When dopamine existed in the solution, dopamine eliminated the photoexcited holes of nanoMoS2 thus avoided electron-hole recombination effectively because the dopamine was oxidized by the photoexcited holes. The hole of nanoMoS2 can be refilled by electrons from the electron-donor and these occupied holes are then ready for the next excitation. As a result, photocurrent was enhanced [32]. The mechanism of this process is shown in Scheme 1. In this produces, the dopamine was oxidized by photoexcited holes into dopaminequinone (DQ). And ultimately, melanin-like compounds are formed [45,46]. As an intended consequence, it was found that the dopamine solution became darker than before performing experiment. Color changed to melanin-like compound which was observed with the naked eyes and the photographs were taken with a Sony Cyber-shot digital camera (Fig. S7). A UV–vis spectrophotometery was carried out to study dopamine solution before and after the experiment. The results showed that there were characteristic absorption peaks at 220 and 281nm of dopamine [47,46]. After experiment, the black product was measured and UV–vis spectral showed no maxima or minima absorption peak which corresponds to the melanin-like compound (Fig. S8) [48]. It suggested that dopamine acted as a sacrificial electron donor to deplete the photogenerated holes. Based on the charge recombination suppression, an enhanced photocurrent strategy is established for the sensitive detection of the dopamine [35].

A sensitive and simple photoelectrochemical platform with nanoMoS2 was fabricated in this paper. The nanoMoS2 was coated on the gold electrode to detect dopamine by using photoelectrochemical platform. Comparing three kinds of nanoMoS2, single-layer nanoMoS2, multiple-layers nanoMoS2 and small-sized nanoMoS2, it was found that single-layer nanoMoS2 performs best PEC response for dopamine detection. Our work will shed light on determination of small molecule by using nanoMoS2-based simple, rapid, sensitive and easy-to-operate photoelectrochemical techniques. It also provided an available method for dopamine in-situ measurement in neurobiology in the further.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575073, 21405088), the Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province, Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (2012G0020221, ZR2014JL013) and the Open Project Program of the Key Laboratory of Marine Bioactive Substance and Modern Analysis Technology, SOA (MBSMAT-2015-04).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.04.065.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Xu Hun was graduated from Shangxi Normal University at China in 2008 and got the Ph. D. Then he worked at Qingdao University of Science and Technology as an associate professor. His research interests are biosensor, optical biosensor, luminescence analysis and their applications in environment and life Science. He has published 50 papers in worldwide journal.

Shanshan Wang received her B.S. degree from Qingdao University of Science and Technology (China) in 2015. She entered the MS course in Qingdao University of Science and Technology (China) in 2015, majored in analytical chemistry. Now, she is engaged in photoelectrochemical and biosensors.

Shiyu Wang received his B.S. degree from Heilongjiang University of Science and Technology (China) in 2014. She entered the MS course in Qingdao University ofScience and Technology (China) in 2014, majored in analytical chemistry. Now, he is engaged in bioanalytical chemistry and biosensors.

Dr. Jikuan Zhao received his PhD degree from Shandong University in 2009. He worked at Qingdao University of Science and Technology as an associate professor from 2002. His research interests are Electrochemistry and Electrochemical analysis.

Dr. Xiliang Luo received his PhD degree from Nanjing University in 2005. He then worked as a postdoctoral fellow in Dublin City University, Arizona State University and the University of Pittsburgh successively. In 2011, he became a research assistant professor in the Department of Bioengineering, University of Pittsburgh and a senior Marie Curie Fellow in the Department of Chemistry, University of Oxford. He then joined the Qingdao University of Science and Technology at the end of 2011 as a Taishan Scholar professor. His scientific interests are focused on biochemical analysis, nanobiosensing, electrochemistry and conducting polymers.
